title: Chapter 15: The Arbiter's Game
content: The fortress gates opened before Roxanne reached them.
Not because she was welcome — because the Arbiter had been watching since she crossed the territorial boundary. The message was clear: I know you're coming. I'm not afraid.
Inside, the fortress was a labyrinth of corridors lined with contract scrolls. Thousands of them. Millions. Every dispute the Arbiter had ever adjudicated, preserved in parchment and shadow.
A servant waited at the entrance. It was a construct — made of paper and ink, its form folding and unfolding as it moved. Its voice was the rustle of pages.
"The Arbiter awaits you in the Hall of Settlement. Follow me."
It led them through the labyrinth, past scrolls that pulsed with trapped souls, past shelves of contracts that stretched into darkness. Roxanne kept her hand on the card in her pocket.

The Hall of Settlement was a circular chamber with a domed ceiling painted to resemble a star chart. In the center stood a woman who looked about forty, with silver-streaked black hair and eyes that held the weary authority of someone who had been right too many times.
The Arbiter.
She wore a robe of deep crimson, embroidered with golden scales. She was not armed. She didn't need to be.
"Roxanne Vance," she said. Her voice was calm. Measured. "The Third Seat sent you."
"He made a suggestion. I made a decision."
"They're the same thing. The old man doesn't suggest. He maneuvers."
She walked around Roxanne, studying her.
"You're young. Human. You've had the card for less than a week. And you think you can take my seat?"
"I don't think. I know."
The Arbiter laughed. It was a genuine sound, warm and surprised.
"I haven't heard that kind of confidence in a thousand years. It's refreshing."
She stopped in front of Roxanne.
"Here's how this works. I don't fight. I arbitrate. So our contest will be a dispute. I will present a case. You will argue it. The one who constructs the stronger argument wins."
"A debate?"

"A contract dispute. But yes, functionally, a debate. The subject: a territorial claim between two entities. One of them is you. The other is me. The territory: the campus you currently hold. I claim that your purchase was invalid because the seller — the prior owner — did not have clear title."
She smiled.

"Defend your ownership."
\n---\n
Roxanne took a moment. The Arbiter watched her with patient amusement.
"You're giving me a case where I have to defend my own purchase against your claim that the seller didn't have clear title."
"Correct."
"And you've been adjudicating disputes for six thousand years."
"Correct."
"So you already know the outcome you want."
"I know the likely outcome. But I'm curious to see how you arrive at it."
Roxanne looked at the scroll again. Then she looked past it — at the card's display, which was showing the Arbiter's own information:

*ARBITER — SECOND SEAT OF THE UPPER CHAMBER*
*— Contract adjudications: 4,782,319*
*— Overturned on appeal: 12*
*— Error rate: 0.00025%*
Twelve overturned cases in six thousand years.
"You've only been wrong twelve times," Roxanne said.
The Arbiter's expression flickered. "You can see my record?"
"The card shows me prices. Including the price of being wrong."
She focused on the twelve overturned cases. Their details appeared in her peripheral vision.
*Case #2,341,008: Territorial dispute. Overturned on appeal because the defendant's claim was invalidated by a prior contract the Arbiter had not been shown.*
*The Arbiter's error: incomplete evidence.*

"The first time you were wrong," Roxanne said slowly, "it was because you didn't have all the information. The appellant presented a forged document. You ruled on it. The fraud wasn't discovered until three centuries later."
The Arbiter's face went still.
"How do you know that?"
"The card shows me everything. Every case. Every ruling. Every mistake."
She smiled.

"You want to test my ability to defend a contract? Fine. But let's make it interesting. Let's talk about case #2,341,008. Because I think the flaw in my campus deed isn't the only document in this room that might not hold up to scrutiny."
A long silence.
Then the Arbiter smiled. A thin, appreciative expression.
"You're cleverer than you look."
"I'm an art student. We're trained to find the flaws in composition."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."

"Debate is about persuasion. The Arbiter has been persuading people for six thousand years. She's better at it than anyone alive. But I don't need to persuade her. I need to prove that the entire system she's built is contingent on my card."
"How?"
"By buying something she can't afford to lose."
She walked to the center of the chamber. The Arbiter watched from her throne of crystallized ink.
"You claim that Marcus Chen didn't have clear title to the campus. I agree. But that's irrelevant. I didn't buy the title from Marcus Chen. I bought the campus from the Nether-Grid itself."
"The Nether-Grid does not sell real estate directly."
"It does when the holder of the Primordial Card makes a direct purchase."
She held up the card. It pulsed. The entire chamber flickered.
*DISPLAY: BLACKWOOD UNIVERSITY — DIRECT NETHE-R-GRID ACQUISITION — PRICE: 10,470,000 OBOLS — STATUS: VALID — SIGNED: PRIMORDIAL COUNCIL CHARTER SECTION 12, SUBSECTION A.*
The Arbiter stared at the display.
"That section doesn't allow direct —"

"It does for Primordial artifact holders. The Charter is older than your position. You've never had occasion to read that section because no Primordial artifact holder has ever made a direct purchase."
"But I have now."
